gaps: Generative eval variance (15-20pt) not surfaced to founder — if score jumps 8pts, founder can't tell if real or resampling noise (file:line eval.sh:500-850)
eval.sh LLM calls have no timeout — could block indefinitely if Claude API hangs with no founder warning (file:line eval.sh:725+)
eval-cache.json corruption (malformed JSON) silently uses old data without alerting founder (file:line score.sh:260-285)
YAML parser doesn't handle quoted values, multiline, or escaping — brittle on edge cases (file:line config.sh:30-75)
Lens extension sourcing (score.sh:97) is failure vector — if a lens-score-*.sh has syntax errors, whole script fails with unclear message
score.sh at 1266 lines needs lib/ decomposition — too dense to audit or maintain (file:line score.sh:1-1266)
min() formula floors score silently — when hygiene<20, score drops but UI doesn't explain 'ONE weak dimension floors you all' (file:line score.sh:1118-1140)
Onboarding mode caps score at 50 — founder can't see real building progress until they write assertions, limits early signal (file:line score.sh:1050-1100)
Cache stale check iterates 9 dirs/files separately — not DRY, brittle on directory structure changes (file:line score.sh:170-195)
No validation of assertion syntax before running — garbage assertions (typos in test names) won't flag, just fail silently
scoring.evidence: Solid foundation for assertion-based product measurement. Core idea (founder's own beliefs determine score) is honest. Execution is professional (caching, error handling, formatting). But rough edges: generative eval variance leaks into the number without confidence intervals, eval.sh hangs on API failure, code is too dense. This ships and works—a good engineer built it—but wouldn't be your reference implementation. The assertion approach is the gem; the execution is 70s-level competent.

gaps: Auto-grading is silent — grade.sh runs with --quiet flag (session_start.sh:322), user sees only aggregated summary, never learns WHICH predictions were graded or WHY

Knowledge model never consolidates — predictions are graded and stored in TSV (grade.sh:205-215 writes model_update), but no script ever feeds these insights back into experiment-learnings.md. The knowledge file remains hand-edited

Claim extraction is brittle — grade_via_signals() uses keyword matching on semi-structured data (grade.sh:122-140), and unmatched predictions auto-expire after 14 days with no human review (grade.sh:150-160). Predictions like 'will improve' don't match patterns like 'raise X from N to M'

Prediction logging is observation-heavy — predictions.tsv contains research observations mixed with testable hypotheses (rows 18-19 are analysis, not future claims). Grading observations as predictions produces false negatives
No decision feedback — graded predictions never change behavior. session_start.sh displays accuracy (360-395), trail.sh renders trends, but /plan and /go never read predictions.tsv to inform strategy

Learning eval self-scores 28/100 (self.sh LEARN_SCORE) — the feature's own diagnostic confirms it's failing. From self.md: 'The learning feature should be the smartest part of the system. It's the worst.'
learning.evidence: This is a half-built logging system pretending to be a learning system. Predictions are recorded, many are auto-graded, and accuracy is displayed. But the core claim—'a model that gets smarter every session'—is false. The feedback loop exists (predict → measure → grade) but stops there. Graded insights never consolidate into the knowledge model, are never fed back into decisions, and remain invisible to the user (--quiet flag). The system's own self-diagnostic admits the gap: learning scores 28/100 because infrastructure exists but learning doesn't actually happen. For delivery: it logs and measures but doesn't compound. For craft: code is readable but UX hides the signal (silent grading), and architecture is incomplete (no consolidation). This is scaffolding that needs two more pieces: (1) make grading visible + consolidate predictions into experiment-learnings.md, (2) wire graded predictions into /plan and /go decision-making so insights actually change behavior.

gaps: Modal routing is underdocumented. /plan, /feature, /ideate, /research each claim 4-8 modes but routing logic is never shown. How does $ARGUMENTS='foo' map to a mode? Exact substring? Feature name? Falls through to default? No code shown. /plan says 'exact keyword wins' but ambiguity persists. (plan/SKILL.md:80-100)

Scripts are completely hidden. Every skill calls bash scripts (session-context.sh, opportunity-scan.sh, bottleneck-report.sh, pre-build-scan.sh, assertion-gate.sh, plateau-check.sh, slop-check.sh, etc.) but none are provided. Skills are specifications that assume perfect scripts exist. If a script returns garbage or doesn't exist, silent failure is most likely. (all files, line counts unknown)

Output templates are hidden behind reference.md files. /plan says 'format per reference.md' and 'output formatting templates'. /go says 'format per reference.md'. /taste references 'templates/taste-report.md'. /rhino references 'templates/dashboard.md'. But these templates are not shown in the code review. Cannot verify output is scannable, voice-compliant, or actually useful to founder. (all files, repeated pattern)

Inter-skill integration is implicit and fragile. /plan references reading 'last-research.yml from /research'. /research says write 'to ~/.claude/cache/last-research.yml for /plan to read'. But there's no explicit error if this file doesn't exist or is stale. No version check. /ideate says 'log to idea-log.json', /go says 'read tasks'. These chains break silently if one skill is skipped. (plan/SKILL.md:185, research/SKILL.md:201, ideate/SKILL.md:290, go/SKILL.md:65)

Tier-based behavior routing creates divergence that cannot be tested. /plan (lines 147-161) changes moves based on 5 tiers. /go (lines 41-58) changes build loop frequency by tier. /taste caps scores by stage. But maturity-tier.sh can fail (script not found, returns empty, returns 'unknown'). No error case. A founder at boundary tier gets inconsistent advice. (plan/SKILL.md:147, go/SKILL.md:41, taste/SKILL.md unknown capping)

Agent error handling is missing. /eval spawns evaluator agents in background (lines 59-62). /go spawns builder agents (line 137). /research spawns market-analyst (line 195). But if an agent times out, fails, or produces bad output: no recovery. Skills continue as if agent succeeded. No validation of agent output format/quality. (eval/SKILL.md:59, go/SKILL.md:137, research/SKILL.md:195)

State file collisions are unhandled. Multiple skills write to .claude/cache/eval-cache.json (eval, /feature reads/writes, /go reads). /rhino snapshots state to rhino-snapshots.json. /research writes to last-research.yml. No locking, conflict detection, or version control. Simultaneous writes risk JSON corruption. (all files write to shared cache)

Script invocations lack error handling. /plan line 134 'Run these via Bash (parallel where possible)' with no error checking shown. /go line 51 'bash scripts/pre-build-scan.sh' with no || exit or fallback. Most Bash calls are bare invocations. If a script returns non-zero, does /plan/go notice or continue with stale data? (plan/SKILL.md:134-140, go/SKILL.md:51, all files)

Plan effectiveness check is vague about control flow. /plan lines 173-183 describe three outcomes (tasks completed+score improved, tasks completed+score flat, tasks incomplete). But then what? If score flat, should /plan re-diagnose or suggest /strategy? No control flow shown. It diagnoses the problem but doesn't say what to do. (plan/SKILL.md:173-183)

Quick-capture mode is documented but implementation is unclear. /plan lines 104-109 claim: 'if $ARGUMENTS contains must/should/always/never → assert, else → task'. But no code shown. How is substring detection implemented? If logic fails, all unrecognized args become tasks, breaking brainstorm/critique modes. (plan/SKILL.md:104-109)

Speculative branching lacks convergence logic. /go lines 137-140 say 'spawn builder per approach in worktrees, compare scores, keep winner'. But how is 'compare' done? Is 65 vs 63 a meaningful difference or noise? No threshold. No tie logic. Mechanism is completely missing. (go/SKILL.md:137-140)

Required file dependencies are implicit, not enforced. /go line 44 says 'prioritize tasks advancing product-spec.yml signals' but assumes product-spec.yml exists. If it doesn't, does /go error? Silently ignore? Behavior is undefined. /eval assumes rubric files exist (cache/rubrics/<feature>.json). Missing files cause graceful degradation but inconsistent behavior. (go/SKILL.md:44, eval/SKILL.md:45-47)

Feature scope routing is underspecified. /go lines 15-20 say '/go [feature]' scopes to feature, multiple features 'work sequentially'. But which feature first? Eval score? Todo count? No priority logic. Single feature scope is clear. Multi-feature scope is ambiguous. (go/SKILL.md:15-20)

Brainstorm mode mechanism is opaque. /plan lines 145-146 document brainstorm: 'skips bottleneck, proposes 5 high-information directions'. But how is this implemented? Does brainstorm call a different script than default bottleneck analysis? Or call same script with different filter? Not shown. Unclear if brainstorm actually differs. (plan/SKILL.md:145-146)

First-run detection creates critical path dependency. /plan line 133 calls 'bash skills/shared/first-run-detect.sh' from plan's SKILL.md but script is in shared/ folder. Legacy installs might not have it. No fallback. If script missing, /plan errors on first invocation. (plan/SKILL.md:133)
commands.evidence: Architecture is modular, tier-aware, and evidence-grounded. Each skill has clear use case and can differentiate behavior by mode/context (good). But the actual implementation is opaque: modal routing is ambiguous, scripts/templates are hidden, inter-skill integration is implicit, error handling is thin. This reads like a specification that assumes perfect dependencies. A founder using these skills would hit missing-script errors, silent failures at integration points, and ambiguous behavior when mode routing fails. Craft is below 50 because state safety is unaddressed, agent error cases are unhandled, and output quality cannot be verified (templates hidden).

gaps: Hooks not configured by install.sh — /go requires post-edit/post_commit hooks for grading, but script checks for hooks.json validity (line 412-420) without integrating into settings.json or Claude Code's hook system. Result: /go command is broken for new users who follow install.sh → claude → /go workflow.

Plugin mode has no fallback if rhino-mind skill fails to load — script assumes plugin system loads skills but doesn't validate they're actually available. Silent failure: user runs /plan and gets 'command not found' with no diagnostic.

No post-install guidance — script exits after setup with no 'next steps' message. README shows boot card and /plan examples, but install.sh doesn't tell user to `claude` in a project or which command to run first. User confusion on what state the system is in.

No end-to-end validation of installed system — --test mode validates individual tools (rhino version, rhino score, rhino todo) but doesn't test that /plan or /eval actually work, which require skills routing or hooks to function.
README mentions `rhino doctor` (line 'rhino doctor # verify everything works') but neither install.sh nor test section validates 'doctor' command exists — documentation mismatch.
install.evidence: Install script successfully creates directories and symlinks, giving the appearance of completeness. But hooks—which /go needs for autonomous prediction grading—are never configured. New user follows install → opens Claude → runs /go → silent failure or incomplete grading. Script validates tools individually but not end-to-end system state. Code is well-structured with good error handling for what it does, but misses the critical integration step (hooks) that makes the advanced features work. Partial delivery, solid craft for the scope attempted.

gaps: cmd_promote() implementation not shown in code extract — cannot verify that promoted todos are written to plan.yml as claimed. This is the critical integration point for the feature claim 'promote' (bin/todo.sh dispatch calls cmd_promote but function body not visible)
cmd_add() not shown — auto-tagging claim ('auto-tags feature from title') in SKILL.md is unverified. Only import logic shown, which is different code path
cmd_done() graduation logic not shown — 'graduation check' claim in SKILL.md not visible in extract. Critical for recurring-to-assertion conversion
Indentation handling assumes spaces only, will break with YAML files using tabs (bin/todo.sh:165 uses match() for indent detection but no tab support)
item_field() and item_set_field() silently return empty on malformed/missing items — no debug output when todo ID doesn't exist, making failures silent and hard to trace
No visible validation that feature tags exist in rhino.yml — SKILL.md claims 'validated against rhino.yml' but no grep/validation shown in code extract
Lock mechanism called (acquire_lock, release_lock) but not shown — suggests concurrent safety intent but implementation missing from visible code

No visible bidirectional integration with plan.yml, go.yml, or strategy.yml despite SKILL.md claiming promote writes to plan.yml. Skills/ folder not shown so can't verify if /plan reads todos.yml or if /go seeds todos.yml
todo.evidence: The visible code (helpers, import, decay, dispatch) is well-structured and correct. File persistence and atomic writes are properly implemented. But the three critical operations that make this feature work — cmd_add, cmd_promote, cmd_done — are not shown in the 300 visible lines of 1304. The code claims 'promote adds to plan.yml' but shows zero code writing to plan.yml. Without seeing the actual implementations, I cannot verify that promote integrates with the planning system, which is essential to the feature claim 'persistent backlog that survives sessions.' The assumption is that the missing 1000 lines contain solid implementations (import suggests they do), but unverified. Tab character bug is confirmed and breaks the feature on non-space-indented YAML. Silent error handling makes debugging difficult.

gaps: Score formula is vague and potentially contradictory: line 172 claims 'your score is the percentage of assertions passing' but line 54 describes '5 measurement tiers... orchestrated... into one number' — unclear how these map or which is primary

Auto-revert is promised (line 8: 'reverts changes that make things worse') but never demonstrated in the walkthrough — line 90 shows '✓ score held' but no example of a regression actually triggering a revert

Predictions are used in walkthrough (line 102: 'predict: contextual output...') before being defined (line 132: 'Every action starts with a prediction') — new reader won't understand what predictions are

First 5 minutes section (line 57-59) says 'Then: 1. What should I work on? → routes to /plan' with no syntax shown — assumes reader knows how to invoke /plan in Claude Code or doesn't show expected output

Assertion auto-generation is implied (line 30: 'generates assertions automatically') but only explained late in /onboard section (line 208-212) — reader doesn't understand how assertions are created during normal usage
No links to deeper documentation, FAQ, or 'Further reading' — README ends at line 222 with vague 'What's next' rather than concrete next steps for learning more
docs.evidence: The README succeeds at explaining WHAT rhino-os is (measurement system for product quality, not code quality) and WHY it matters (AI tools are less productive without measurement). It shows real examples and honest limitations. But it fails at explaining HOW to use it end-to-end: the score formula is stated two different ways without reconciliation, auto-revert is claimed but never shown working, key concepts (predictions, assertion auto-generation) are used before definition, and the first 5 minutes section omits syntax and expected output. For a feature whose entire claim is 'clear explanation of what rhino-os is and how to use it,' these are significant gaps.
